html:
Renascience Inc. (4889 JP)

Current price: ¥1,905 at the May 15, 2026 TSE close; PTS last was ¥1,955. Market cap is about ¥26.2 billion, cash is ¥3.4 billion, and net cash-adjusted enterprise value is roughly ¥22.8 billion.

The old glaucoma/ALS hardware thesis should be discarded. Renascience is a Tohoku University-origin biotech built around RS5614, an oral PAI-1 inhibitor, with smaller SaMD and medical-device monetization paths around it. The financials look ugly on a screen: FY3/2026 operating revenue was only ¥68.6 million, operating loss was ¥356.9 million, and the FY3/2027 guide calls for only ¥43 million of revenue and a ¥585 million operating loss in the latest company results. That is exactly why the name is interesting. Reported revenue is not the asset. The asset is a funded, multi-shot clinical platform that the market still treats like a speculative Japanese biotech press-release machine.

The core variant perception is that Renascience is not randomly spraying indications. It is trying to prove one mechanistic idea: PAI-1 is involved in cancer immune escape, fibrosis, cellular senescence, and treatment resistance, and an oral inhibitor can be layered onto existing therapies without the toxicity burden of another antibody or cytotoxic regimen. If that is even partly right, RS5614 is not a single rare-cancer drug. It is a low-cost oral enhancer that can be licensed indication by indication.

The best current asset is CML. In late Phase II, RS5614 plus TKIs produced a 33.3% deep molecular response rate at 48 weeks versus historical TKI-alone controls of 8% to 12%, and the company reports no serious treatment-related adverse events after one year. The ongoing Phase III is designed around sustained DMR and treatment-free remission. That matters commercially because TKIs keep many CML patients alive but often lock them into long, expensive therapy. A drug that helps patients stop TKIs is payer-friendly, patient-friendly, and easy to understand.

Melanoma is the second late-stage leg. RS5614 is in a Japan Phase III trial with nivolumab for unresectable melanoma, and a Taiwan bridging study began in December 2025. Phase II showed a 25.9% response rate and 66.7% disease-control rate in nivolumab-refractory melanoma, with a safety profile that looks much cleaner than the nivolumab plus ipilimumab approach. Japan is a small melanoma market, but the strategic value is broader: positive Phase III data would validate RS5614 as an oral checkpoint-resistance modifier.

The rest of oncology supplies torque. Angiosarcoma Phase II data showed disease stabilization in 13 of 15 evaluable patients, 4.0 months PFS and 20.8 months OS versus Japanese pazopanib prospective-trial comparators of 2.8 months and 12.1 months. NSCLC Phase II was mixed but useful: overall ORR was only 8.3%, but the third-line subgroup was 18.2%, and the signal was strong enough for an AMED-supported locally advanced NSCLC Phase II. Pancreatic cancer just started Phase II on May 12, 2026, combining RS5614 with gemcitabine plus nab-paclitaxel in 50 patients. Any one of these can fail without killing the thesis, but each positive readthrough raises the value of the same molecule.

The new speculative accelerant is longevity. The company's XPRIZE Healthspan semifinal data were open-label, tiny, and not approvable evidence, but they were exactly the kind of data that can move a small-cap biotech: 19 evaluable older adults, no serious safety issue, and a reported 2 to 3 year improvement in epigenetic age markers after four months. If Renascience is selected as a TOP 10 finalist in August 2026, the stock can trade less like a Japanese rare-oncology name and more like a global longevity call option. The regulatory path for anti-aging remains unclear, but markets pay for category creation well before they pay for reimbursement clarity.

There is also a quieter business transition underneath. The AI hemodialysis SaMD program achieved its clinical performance endpoint, then Nipro extended the joint-development arrangement and paid an upfront fee. The disposable ultrafine endoscope is already approved in Japan and licensed to HI-LEX Medical. These are not enough to justify the company alone, but they validate Renascience's model: use university networks, public funding, and industry partners to move assets farther than its own P&L could support.

The balance sheet gives the stock time. Cash of ¥3.4 billion compares with FY3/2026 operating cash burn of ¥291 million and a FY3/2027 guided net loss of ¥548 million. Dilution risk is real, especially if management widens the clinical slate, but this is not a biotech facing immediate financing death. At the current share count of 13.78 million, cash is about ¥247 per share.

The bear case is obvious. SSc-ILD missed its primary endpoint. Many Phase II datasets are open-label or small. The XPRIZE data are not placebo-controlled. The stock has become a retail momentum vehicle, with Yahoo Japan board sentiment overwhelmingly bullish and May 15 volume spiking to 2.7 million shares. If the longevity story cools and Phase III readouts disappoint, the stock can give back most of the recent move.

But the upside is asymmetric because the enterprise value is still only about ¥23 billion. That is not a demanding price for two Phase III oncology programs, multiple Phase II expansion shots, an oral senolytic narrative, and partnerable SaMD assets. Renascience is not cheap on trailing revenue or PBR. It is cheap on risk-adjusted forward asset value if even one of CML, melanoma, or longevity becomes externally partnerable.

My targets using the ¥1,905 May 15 close:

Bear: ¥800, down 58%. Phase III disappointment, XPRIZE fade, retail unwind, and the stock trades toward cash plus discounted IP.

Base: ¥3,500, up 84%. One late-stage oncology program continues to de-risk, SaMD commercialization with Nipro becomes visible, and the market assigns real option value to RS5614 beyond Japan.

Bull: ¥7,500, up 294%. CML or melanoma data support approval or a licensing deal, RS5614 becomes recognized as an oral immuno-oncology enhancer, and XPRIZE finalist selection pulls global longevity investors into a ¥100 billion-plus equity-value framework.
